utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: so donna is a 37 -year-old female she is here with complaint of headache she has a history of hypertension and diabetes so donna tell me what's going on

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah lately i've just been having quite a bit of a headache and i mean every now and then i'll get it but lately it's just been getting worse and i've noticed that my blood pressure has been running high i've been taking it maybe like once a week but the past three weeks it's been really high than usual so i was a little worried

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: do you remember the numbers that you're getting on your blood pressure readings

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah so one of them was like one sixty over eighty seven and then the last one was like one seventy five over ninety two

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay so it's really high i was looking back and it looks like your blood pressure was pretty normal last time we saw you is one twenty four over eighty two have you been able to take your blood pressure medicine the lisinopril that you've been prescribed

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i have but sometimes i forget i'm i'm embarrassed to tell you that but yeah sometimes i forget i get busy like especially on the weekends i'm very good during the week but on the weekends i tend to forget it and that's when i'm most busy too

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: yeah well i'm also curious you know you have diabetes have you been checking your blood sugar

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah i have because i thought maybe you know that's why i'm getting the headaches maybe my sugar was too low or maybe too high but my sugar has been okay it's been running like one twenty sometimes it does get down to like one hundred

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay and have you noticed are you urinating more than usual or anything like that

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: not really so much it's it's been pretty much the same but pretty normal

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay and when you get these headaches what are you taking for them

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: well i take my tylenol hoping it'll just you know go away and then it does some just for a little bit but then it it just goes throughout the day and i start to get them again

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay and when you first had the headaches what would you rate the headache at zero no pain ten the worst pain ever

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i would say when i first get them it's about i would say a seven out of ten

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: and then after you take the tylenol what does it go down to

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: it goes down to probably about a four maybe five it i still feel it i still feel annoying but it's not as pounding as as it normally would be

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: if i did n't take the tylenol

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: yeah and have you ever had headaches like this before

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: not like this you know you know as you know sometimes i get migraine but i know i never really take anything for it that's like once a year or once or every two years but not like not like this one headaches like this one

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay and have you had any congestion anything like that cough runny nose

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah i thought maybe because you know now it's springtime and i tend to get my allergies but it it's it's very different and i've not actually had any like runny nose or cough or congestion along with the headache

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay if you tap on your forehead do you have any tenderness

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: right here

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: yeah

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: just a little not really too much

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay if you tap down here on these bones your cheek bones any tenderness there

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: a little bit not as much as i guess if i tap up here

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay and then if you kinda push on your neck around here any tenderness

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: it it's a little tender but it's not it is a little bit i'd say

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay okay so you do n't have any frontal sinus tenderness no maxillary sinus tenderness it sounds like you do n't have any lymphadenopathy that would just be if your lymph nodes were tender so yeah you know let's just talk about a few things that i wan na do and then i want you to actually come in and and be seen by doctor ruth so first off your headache i think taking tylenol is great you can keep taking that you know your lisinopril you're on twenty milligrams once a day which is a medium dose but i think we can increase that so for your hypertension and your headaches i'm gon na have you increase your lisinopril to forty milligrams once a day

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: and then i want you to keep checking your blood pressure and i wan na order some blood tests i'd like to get some just normal blood work we'll get a hemoglobin a1c some laboratory tests things like that and and then for your diabetes i'm gon na have some diabetic education for you that i want you to be able to review it sounds like your blood sugar has been running a little bit high and after you get the blood tests after that's all done i want you to come in to see me or doctor ruth and we'll just make sure that everything is okay that we get your blood pressure under control any questions for me

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no i mean i've been walking and i know i get the headaches but is it alright if i still continue my walks

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: yeah that's great i think exercise is wonderful for everything going on go ahead and walk still

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay great we'll do that thank you

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: you're welcome thanks so much donna

detail_list.summary: The patient is a 37-year-old female with a history of hypertension and diabetes presenting with a headache.
Headache
The patient reports worsening headaches and elevated home blood pressure readings over the past three weeks.
The doctor asks the patient for her specific blood pressure readings.
The patient reports home blood pressure readings of 160/87 and 175/92.
The doctor notes a previous blood pressure reading of 124/82.
The doctor asks if the patient has been taking her prescribed lisinopril.
The patient admits to occasionally forgetting to take her lisinopril, particularly on weekends.
Diabetes
The doctor asks if the patient has been monitoring her blood sugar.
The patient reports blood sugar levels ranging between 100 and 120.
The doctor asks if the patient is experiencing polyuria.
The patient denies polyuria.
The doctor asks what the patient takes for her headaches.
The patient reports taking Tylenol for headache pain, which provides only temporary relief.
The doctor asks the patient to rate the severity of her headaches on a scale of zero to ten.
The patient rates her initial headache pain as a seven out of ten.
The doctor asks how the headache pain changes after taking Tylenol.
The patient reports that Tylenol reduces her headache pain to a four or five out of ten.
The doctor asks if the patient has experienced similar headaches in the past.
The patient reports a history of rare migraines occurring once every one to two years, but states these current headaches are different.
The doctor asks if the patient has symptoms of congestion, cough, or runny nose.
The patient denies cough, runny nose, or congestion, despite a history of seasonal allergies.
The doctor asks if the patient has frontal sinus tenderness.
The patient reports minimal frontal sinus tenderness.
The doctor asks if the patient has maxillary sinus tenderness.
The patient reports minimal maxillary sinus tenderness.
The doctor asks if the patient has neck tenderness.
The patient reports slight neck tenderness.
The doctor confirms no frontal or maxillary sinus tenderness and no lymphadenopathy.
The doctor increases the patient's lisinopril dosage to 40 mg once a day.
The doctor orders blood work, including a hemoglobin A1c, and provides diabetic education materials.
The patient is instructed to return for a follow-up visit with the doctor or Dr. Ruth to review lab results.
The patient asks if she can continue her walking exercise program.
The doctor encourages the patient to continue her walking program.
